verdicts.interest_take: Forgiveness reframed as defiance, not weakness — the episode's clearest ethical statement and a real 'I felt that' beat.
verdicts.writing_notes: Idea is the true payoff and distinct from moment/why; warm, concrete, no dialogue reproduced. Best-written card in the set.

verdicts.interest_take: The title made literal, plus the genuine Chinese folklore most viewers assume was invented for the gag — strong 'I never caught that.'
verdicts.writing_notes: Tight; idea carries the folklore payoff and the why ties it to the team. Keeps the Roy gag tasteful.

verdicts.interest_take: Jamie choosing connector over finisher is the human form of the whole theme; measured against S1 ego, it lands.
verdicts.writing_notes: Clear arc framing; idea distinct from moment. Slightly arc-dependent but reads fine on its own.

verdicts.interest_take: Real LeBron/Ingraham origin behind Sam's storyline is a sharp non-fiction hook a casual viewer would miss.
verdicts.writing_notes: Crisp; idea delivers the real-world source, why literalizes it on the wall. Right category (Ethics).

verdicts.interest_take: Total Football as ethic-not-formation is the episode's tactical spine; Michels/Cruyff history is worth knowing.
verdicts.writing_notes: A touch teachy but well-organized; the moral gloss is the payoff. Necessary context for the red-string and Jamie cards.

verdicts.interest_take: The strings that bind in training become the bond around a wounded teammate — the off-pitch payoff of the title.
verdicts.writing_notes: Warm and clear; idea ties pitch to community. Pairs with forgive-them without simply repeating it.

verdicts.interest_take: Rebecca naming Jack's gifts as 'love bombing' recasts the romance as a warning — gives the set a relationship beat outside the two big plots.
verdicts.writing_notes: Idea distinct from moment; the Rupert echo is the real weight. Adds variety to a tactics-heavy episode.

verdicts.interest_take: An Afrobeats needle-drop answering a racist attack with Sam's own Nigerian identity — quiet, easy-to-miss craft.
verdicts.writing_notes: Lone aesthetics card; concise and pointed. Good closer that rewards a re-listen.

verdicts.interest_take: Trent narrating 'this took three seasons' is the episode explaining itself — the most recap-ish restatement of an interconnection theme the red-string and Jamie cards already deliver more vividly.
verdicts.writing_notes: Well-written but redundant; fourth card on the same selflessness/connection idea. spoiler_safety 3 (leans on prior seasons) seals it.

verdicts.interest_take: Lowest interest (3) and overlaps shut-up-and-dribble's real-world hook without adding emotion or a 'never caught that.'
verdicts.writing_notes: Hedged, critic-reading prose ('commentators read,' 'not a writers' statement') is exactly the academic tone to avoid for a warm reader.

verdicts.interest_take: Neat continuity but thinly sourced (partial) and overlaps the stronger Jamie and Total Football cards; dilutes rather than adds.
verdicts.writing_notes: Fine prose, but the payoff (Jamie has lived this system) is already implied by the heart-not-head card.

episode_notes: Strong episode with a clear spine but real thematic crowding: five cards orbit selflessness/interconnection (Total Football, red string, Jamie, Trent's trust speech) and five touch the Sam plot. Kept the most vivid of each cluster and cut the restatements. Trust-built (Trent) cut as the fourth, most recap-ish version of the connection thesis (and series-dependent). Brinda-barot cut as the lowest-interest, most hedged/academic card, overlapping shut-up-and-dribble. Cruyff-arena cut as thin, partial continuity already implied by the Jamie card. Excluded set reviewed: jane-austen (surprise 5) is genuinely fun but correctly held — contested E6-vs-E7 placement; red-string-improv-origin is single-source (grounding 2). No rescues. Resulting set of 8 is varied: ethics, folklore, arc, real-world reference, tactics, community, relationships, aesthetics — ordered emotional-spine-first, then the title and the marquee arc.
